revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Dei of Wa]][[ja:禰]][[ko:예 (왜왕)]]
{{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|occupation={{ill|ruler|qid=Q1097498|1=ruler}}}}

{{Short description|King of Wakoku, 4th-century Japan|qq=}}
{{tone|date=November 2023|qq=}}

'''{{nihongo|Dei of Wa|禰|qq=}}''', also known as {{Nihongo|2=祖禰|3=Sodei|4={{lit|great ancestor|qq=}}|qq=}}.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Kishimoto |first=Naofumi |date=2013-05-01 |title=Dual Kingship in the Kofun Period as Seen from the Keyhole Tombs |journal=Journal of Urban Culture Research|s2cid=193255655 |qq=}}</ref>{{rp||pages=11|qq=}} was a [[Okimi|King]] of {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|qid=Q10888424|lt=Wakoku|1=Wakoku}}, who was compared to {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|qid=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}}, founder of the Kawachi dynasty by the historian Hidehiro Okada.<ref>岡田, 2008</ref> He was not among the [[Five kings of Wa]] but would be another King of {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|qid=Q10888424|lt=Wa|1=Wakoku}} predating them.<ref name=":1" />{{rp||pages=11|qq=}}
He is sometimes identified with “Mye (彌),” the Wa leader mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Liang|en|Book of Liang|qid=Q1285767|lt=Book of Liang|1=Book of Liang}},<ref name=":1" />{{rp||pages=11|qq=}}
Kuranishi Yūko identifies him as the father of [[Sai of Wa]] who she identifies with {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|qid=Q349258|lt=Emperor Ingyō|1=Emperor Ingyō}}.<ref name=":1" />{{rp||pages=11|qq=}} Okada identifies him with {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|qid=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}}, the grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|qid=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}}.<ref name=":2" />
Some dispute the identification as an individual and take the phrase Sodei to refer to ancestors as a whole.<ref name=":1" />{{rp||pages=11|qq=}}
== Basis for the name ==
This is the genealogy proposed by Okada{{Tree chart/start| summary=Boxes and lines diagram with 6 boxes|qq=}}
{{tree chart| Nin | | | | | | | | |Nin=Dei of Wa（{{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|qid=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}}）|qq=}}
{{tree chart| |!| | | | | | | | | |qq=}}
{{tree chart| Ric | | | | | | | | |Ric={{ill|San of Wa|en|San of Wa|qid=Q28692297|lt=San of Wa|1=San of Wa}}（{{ill|Emperor Richū|en|Emperor Richū|qid=Q329704|lt=Emperor Richū|1=Emperor Richū}}）|qq=}}
{{tree chart| |)|-|-|-|.| | | | | |qq=}}
{{tree chart| Han | | Ing | | | | |Han={{ill|Chin of Wa|en|Chin of Wa|qid=Q28692298|lt=Chin of Wa|1=Chin of Wa}}（{{ill|Emperor Hanzei|en|Emperor Hanzei|qid=Q329769|lt=Emperor Hanzei|1=Emperor Hanzei}}）|Ing=[[Sai of Wa]]（{{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|qid=Q349258|lt=Emperor Ingyō|1=Emperor Ingyō}}）|qq=}}
{{tree chart| | | | | |)|-|-|-|.| |qq=}}
{{tree chart| | | | | Ank | | Yur |Ank=[[Kō of Wa|Ko of Wa]]（{{ill|Emperor Ankō|en|Emperor Ankō|qid=Q361010|lt=Emperor Ankō|1=Emperor Ankō}}）|Yur={{ill|Bu of Wa|en|Bu of Wa|qid=Q28692301|lt=Bu of Wa|1=Bu of Wa}}（{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|qid=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}}）|qq=}}

{{tree chart|CLEAR=Clear box|RED=Red box|GREEN=Green box|BLUE=Blue box|boxstyle_RED=color:white; background-color: red;|boxstyle_GREEN=color:yellow; background-color: green;|boxstyle_BLUE=color:white; background-color: blue;|qq=}}
{{Tree chart/end|qq=}}
The name '''Dei''' is recorded in the {{ill|Book of Song|en|Book of Song|qid=Q1143142|lt=Book of Song|1=Book of Song}} as the Japanese king Wangmu<ref>ワカタケル大王。『日本書紀』にいう雄略天皇</ref> to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|en|Liu Song dynasty|qid=Q49697|lt=Song dynasty|1=Liu Song dynasty}} of the {{ill|Northern and Southern dynasties|en|Northern and Southern dynasties|qid=Q49696|lt=Northern and Southern dynasties|1=Northern and Southern dynasties}} of China in 487,<ref>岡田, 2008, pp. 225, 257</ref>
Okada (岡田) said of {{Nihongo|2=祖禰|3=Sodei|qq=}}
{{Nihongo|2=祖禰|3=Sodei|qq=}} is  {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|qid=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}}, the grandfather of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|qid=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}}.<ref name=":2">岡田, 2008, p. 225、「中国の古典の用例では、「禰」は父の霊をまつる廟を指すので、これまでの学説では、「祖禰」は「祖先以来」を意味するものと漠然と解釈されてきたが、それは間違いである。明確の仁徳天皇の事績を伝えようとしているのだ」</ref>。
{{ill|Bu of Wa|en|Bu of Wa|qid=Q28692301|lt=Bu of Wa|1=Bu of Wa}}, wrote in a letter addressed to the {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|qid=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty}} in China in 487 that he had been engaged in warfare both at sea and abroad since the generation of Grandfather-Dei「祖禰」or his grandfather Dei of Wa. There must be another King of Wa before {{ill|San of Wa|en|San of Wa|qid=Q28692297|lt=San|1=San of Wa}}, {{ill|Chin of Wa|en|Chin of Wa|qid=Q28692298|lt=Chin|1=Chin of Wa}}, and [[Sai of Wa|Sai]].<ref name=":0">岡田, 2008, p. 257</ref>
== See also ==
* {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|qid=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}}
* {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|qid=Q10888424|lt=Wakoku|1=Wakoku}}
* [[Five kings of Wa]]
== References ==
<references />
== Sources ==
* 岡田英弘『倭国』{{ill|Chuokoron-Shinsha|en|Chuokoron-Shinsha|qid=Q996095|lt=中央公論社|1=Chuokoron-Shinsha}},1977
* 岡田英弘『日本史の誕生』{{ill|Chikuma Shobō|en|Chikuma Shobō|qid=Q2963556|lt=筑摩書房|1=Chikuma Shobō}},2008
{{Yamatai|qq=}}
